primer.markdown:
# Prompts are not strings. Prompts are typed arrows.

The first move of `categorical-meta-prompting` is to stop treating a prompt as `String` and start treating it as a morphism — a typed arrow between context objects. In the framework's central functor `F: Task → Prompt`, a task is an object with structure (keywords, modifiers, operators), and a prompt is an object with structure (`{context}`, `{mode}`, `{format}`, assembled body). `F` is the structure-preserving map between them.

The guide decomposes `F` further: `F = F_template ∘ F_tier ∘ F_domain`. Raw task keywords flow through a domain classifier, then a tier selector (`L1..L7` complexity), then a template assembler. Each step is its own arrow. Composition means: running the three sub-functors in order produces the same prompt as running the composite `F` — that is exactly the composition law `F(g ∘ f) = F(g) ∘ F(f)` applied to your concrete pipeline.

Why this buys you something: once prompts are arrows between typed objects, you get *composition for free*. `A → B → C` means run the first prompt whose output becomes the input context for the second, whose output becomes input for the third, and the quality of the composite is `min(q_A, q_B, q_C)` — the weakest link. Parallel prompts `A || B` aggregate as `mean`. You never have to invent this bookkeeping; it falls out of the algebra. The `/meta` command is the executable form of `F`; when you type `/meta "implement rate limiter"`, the framework is quite literally evaluating a functor application.
primer.key_insight: A prompt is a morphism between typed context objects, not a string. Composition, identity, and quality-tracking then fall out of the algebra instead of being bolted on.

visual.alt: Diagram showing the decomposition of F: Task → Prompt as three stacked arrows: F_domain (keywords to domain), F_tier (complexity level L1-L7), F_template (context + mode + format assembly). On the right, a composition diagram shows two prompts f: Task → Prompt and g: Prompt → Response composing as g ∘ f: Task → Response, with the quality rule min(q_f, q_g) labelled beneath.

sandbox_policy.csp: default-src 'none'; style-src 'unsafe-inline'; script-src 'unsafe-inline'; img-src data:; font-src https://fonts.gstatic.com; connect-src 'none'; frame-ancestors 'self'

interactive.success_criterion: Learner enters a task description, selects a tier (L1-L7) and a domain (ALGORITHM | API | SECURITY | DEBUG | TESTING), and the simulator renders the resulting assembled prompt structure `{context:...}+{mode:...}+{format:...} body`. The simulator then shows the functor laws visibly: toggling tier twice returns to the original prompt (identity), and composing two tier transitions equals the single composite transition (composition).

artifact.excerpt:
Task ──[F: Functor]──> Prompt ──[M: Monad]──> Refined ──[W: Comonad]──> Output
        Structure        │        Iteration      │        Context
        Preserving       │        & Refinement   │        Extraction
                        │                        │
                 ┌──────▼────────┐      ┌───────▼────────┐
                 │ Quality [0,1] │      │ Token Budget   │
                 │ Enrichment    │      │ Tracking       │
                 └───────────────┘      └────────────────┘

/<command> @modifier:value [composition] "task description"

Example:
/meta @mode:active @tier:L5 [R→D→I→T] "build auth system"

questions.prompt: The guide decomposes F: Task → Prompt as F_template ∘ F_tier ∘ F_domain. If composition is associative, which of the following rewrites is guaranteed to produce the same prompt?
questions.explanation: Associativity says `(F_template ∘ F_tier) ∘ F_domain = F_template ∘ (F_tier ∘ F_domain)`. Both groupings produce the same prompt because the framework requires F to satisfy `F(g ∘ f) = F(g) ∘ F(f)` (`CATEGORICAL-ONTOLOGY.md §2.1`). The parentheses only change evaluation order, not the result.

questions.prompt: You compose two prompts sequentially: `A → B` where `q(A) = 0.9` and `q(B) = 0.7`. What is the quality of the composite under the framework's rule?
questions.explanation: Sequential composition uses `min`: `q(A → B) = min(q_A, q_B) = min(0.9, 0.7) = 0.7`. This is stated in `docs/CATEGORICAL-META-PROMPTING-COMPLETE-GUIDE.md §Composition Operators` (line ~97). Averaging (0.8) would be the rule for `||` parallel composition, not sequence.

questions.prompt: Why does treating a prompt as a string (rather than a typed morphism) break the composition law?
questions.explanation: When every value is `String`, every arrow is `String → String` and there is no type-level constraint on what can compose with what. The composition law `F(g ∘ f) = F(g) ∘ F(f)` becomes vacuous because there is nothing distinguishing `f` from `g`. Typed objects (`Task`, `Prompt`, `Context`) make the law a real, load-bearing structural claim — without them, composition degenerates to concatenation.
